content.full_text:
Re:
Fern P'olrrrcAr- PRACTTcES C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 t 428 J Street r Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 e Fax (916) 322-0886
August 20,2001
Evan E. Evans
California Department of Transportation
Division of Structure Maintenance
and Investigations
1801 3Oth Street, MS9-1/91
Sacramento, CA 95816
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-01-143
Dear Mr. Evans
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because your question does
not concern a specilc governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance.2
QUESTTON
As a retired annuitant working on an hourly basis with the Caltrans Division of
Structure Maintenance and Investigations, will you violate any conflict of interest laws
under the Act if you accept employment as an independent contractor with a
Massachusetts consulting firm providing additional, but different, consultant services to
the Division of Structure Maintenance and Investigations?
CONCLUSION
The Act does not bar public officials from maintaining outside employment
during their tenure as public officials. However, a conflict of interest under the Act can
arise within the context of specific governmental decisions with reasonably foreseeable,
material financial effects on one or more of an official's economic interests. You have an
economic interest in Cambridge Systematics as a source of income or of promised
1 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
1 8 1 09- 1 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide tlte requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal r,vritten advice (Government Code $ 83114; 2 Ca1. Code of Regs. $ 18329(c)(3).)


File No. I-01-143
Page No. 2
income and possibly as an employee, officer or director of that business. If your
economic interests are implicated in a governmental decision in your capacity as a public
offlcial, they may involve a conflict of interest, as that term is defined by the Act. You
have provided no facts to analyze a specific prospective factual situation.
FACTS
You were a permanent full-time employee of the State of California's Department
of Transportation ("CalTrans") from February 1,1961, until you retired on May 16,
2000 Your last appointment before retirement was as Senior Bridge Engineer
(Computer Applications Specialist) on March 1,, 1992, with the Division of Structure
Maintenance and Investigations. Since your retirement, you have been working on an
hourly basis as a retired annuitarrtwith the Division of Structure Maintenance and
Investigations. As a retired annuitant, you are continuing to perform the duties of Senior
Bridge Engineer (Computer Applications Specialist).
Your specific duties since March l, 1992, have been to maintain an ad hoc system
for generating reports from the database, to keep the information on your department's
Internet home page current, and to occasionally assist the users of your department's on-
line inspection report transmittal systems (Smart). You also perform various
"housekeeping" functions on your department's database that are outside the specific
duties of the DBA assigned to you by the Division of Information Services.
Cambridge Systematics, Inc., a consulting firm based in Cambridge,
Massachusetts, has expressed interest in adding you to a team of application developers
and database design specialists who are currently providing consultant services to the
CalTrans Division of Structure Maintenance and Investigations.
Cambridge Systematics, Inc. is currently providing consultant services to the
Division of Structure Maintenance and Investigations for application development related
to the second generation (Smart2K) of your "Smart" system under a consultant contract
that was competitively bid and awarded. You were not named in Cambridge
Systematics' proposal, nor did Cambridge Systematics contemplate using your skills as a
member of its team at the time it submitted its proposal. You were not involved in the
evaluation of proposals or in any decision-making role in the awarding of this contract.
ANALYSIS
As an employee of the State of California's Department of Transportation, you
are considered a public offrcial for purposes of the disqualification provisions of the Act.
(Section 87103.)
The Act does not prohibit a public offrcial from accepting employment with
employers outside of government. There are, of course, other bodies of law that
sometimes restrict such employment, the law governing "Incompatible Activities of State
Officers and Employees," for example, as well as contractual or other restrictions that


a
File No. I-01-143
Page No. 3
may be imposed by particular employers.' The Commission cannot offer advice in those
areas, which should be addressed by the Office of the Attorney General or by CalTrans's
own counsel. In this letter, we limit our advice to the requirements of the Political
Reform Act, and specifically to the Act's conflict of interest provisions.
Although the Act does not bar a public offrcial from holding any form of private-
sector employment, its conflict of interest rules prohibit (with limited exceptions) any
public official from making, parlicipating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. (Section 87100). The conflict of interestrules are therefore purely situational.
While your inquiry is a general one, made without reference to any specific
"governmental decision," the focus of your concern is plainly the potential for your
anticipated employment relationship to create conflicts of interest.
A public official has a disqualifying financial interest in a governmental decision,
within the meaning of Section 87100, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family.
There are six kinds of economic interests from which conflicts of interest may
arise. They are defined in Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, and include:
o An interest in a business entity in which a public offrcial has a direct or indirect
investmenta of $2,000 or more. (S ection 87 I 03 (a), Regulation I 8703 . 1 (a). )
. Any business entity in which a public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d), Regulation
87103 1(b) )
. An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect interest
of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.)
. Any source of income, including promised income, to the public official which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 1 03 (c), Regulation 1 8703.3.)
. Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregateto $3205 or more within
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e), Regulation 18703.4.)
o A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family, a.k.a. the
"personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5.)
3 CalTrans's own "incompatible activities" policy, which could aflect your outside employment
arrangements, would be explained and interpreted by CalTrans' counsel.
a Section 87103 defines an "indirect investment'' or interest as "any investment or interest owned
by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public offrcial, or by a
business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater."
s The gift limit amount is adjusted biermially by the Commission to reflect changes in the
Consumer Price Index. As of January 1, 2001, the amount is $320. (Section 87103(e), Regulation
18940.2.)


File No. I-01-143
Page No. 4
We presumethat Cambridge Systematics is a source of income or a source of
promised income to you totaling at least $500 over any l2-month period. Therefore, you
have an economic interest in Cambridge Systematics for purposes of Section 87103(c).
Depending on your legal relationship with Cambridge Systematics, you may also have an
economic interest in the company as a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or as a
person holding a position of management. (Section 87103(b).)
At this stage, we can say that there is a potential for a conflict of interest at any
time when you assume a governmental decisionmaking role, where it is possible that the
decision(s) in question will have a material financial effect on your personal finances or
on Cambridge Systematics. It would then be necessary to determine whether you were
indeed making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision, whether
it was foreseeable that the decision(s) would impact one of these economic interests, and
whether any such "tmpact" would amount to a"fftaterial financial effect," as defined by
statute and regulation. (Regulations 18700-18708.) We stress, however, that all of these
elements must be found before a "conflict" is established. The existence of outside
economic interests, alone, does not constitute a conflict of interest for a public offrcial,
within the meaning of the Act.
If at any time you believe that you may have af:-ll,ancial interest in a specific,
upcoming governmental decision, please feel free to contact me for further advice at
(et6) 322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
By: Holly
Armstrong
Staff Counsel, Legal
Enclosure
HBA:jg
